_meta.purpose: Data source for the agent catalog on index.html. Each function has a box on the home page; clicking it reveals its agents as expandable cards. Claude Code renders each card from the locked template (agent-card-preview-v2.html): collapsed shows name + tier badge + the agent-owns/human-keeps split; expanded shows tabs Today, Tomorrow (w/ agent), and Payoff. Show the Payoff tab only when payoff.defensible is true; otherwise render just Today and Tomorrow.

tiers.proof_runnable: built reference agent with a runnable repo
tiers.proof_candidate: strong next build, not yet built — card must not claim a repo or live demo

_meta.payoff_assumptions: All payoff figures are illustrative and deliberately conservative; assumptions are listed per card so a reader can swap in their own rate, volume, and time. Loaded rates used: engineering ~$120/hr, counsel/contract-manager ~$120/hr, analyst/ops ~$80/hr, support ~$50/hr. Where value is mostly judgment leverage or risk reduction rather than a clean time x rate figure, defensible is false and the Payoff tab is omitted.
_meta.boundary_note_rule: Every 'tomorrow' string ends with the deliberate human-gate boundary. Keep it — it is the thesis.

agents.name: Requirements Synthesis Agent
agents.agent_owns: Turning interviews, tickets, and threads into a structured, traceable PRD and flagging contradictions.
agents.today: A PM stitches a PRD together by hand from interview notes, Slack threads, and half-remembered hallway conversations. Requirements get lost, and two stakeholders quietly asking for opposite things isn't noticed until build.
agents.tomorrow: The agent ingests the raw inputs and returns a structured PRD with every requirement traced to its source, plus an explicit list of where stakeholders contradict each other. The PM starts from a clean draft instead of a blank page. The agent drafts and flags; the PM owns what's in scope and what ships first.

payoff.headline: ~6-10 PM hours reclaimed per major spec
assumptions: First-pass PRD assembly and reconciliation ~1 day of PM time today
Agent draft cuts that to ~2-3 hours of editing
not_captured: Contradictions caught pre-build, before they become rework
Traceability that survives an audit or a re-org

agents.name: Spec-to-Backlog Agent
agents.agent_owns: Decomposing a PRD into epics and tickets with acceptance criteria.
agents.today: Someone manually breaks a PRD into epics and tickets, writing acceptance criteria from scratch, often inconsistently across the team.
agents.tomorrow: The agent decomposes the approved PRD into epics and tickets with draft acceptance criteria in a consistent format. The PM reviews and signs off before anything enters a sprint.

payoff.headline: ~3-5 hours saved per PRD
not_captured: Fewer mid-sprint 'what did this ticket mean' stalls

agents.name: Competitive Signal Agent
agents.agent_owns: Tracking competitor releases and pricing, mapping each to a roadmap gap or a deliberate dismissal.
agents.human_keeps: Whether it means anything, and whether to react.
agents.today: Competitive tracking is somebody's side task, done in bursts before planning. Real moves get noticed late.
agents.tomorrow: The agent watches competitor releases, changelogs, and pricing continuously and maps each to a specific place in your roadmap: gap, parity, or dismissed-on-purpose. The PM decides what actually warrants a response.

payoff.note: Value is timing and judgment, not a clean time-times-rate figure. Better framed as risk reduction than hours saved.

agents.name: Roadmap Prioritization Agent
agents.agent_owns: Scoring backlog items against OKRs and capacity, and explaining the ranking.
agents.human_keeps: The final call on what makes the cut.
agents.today: Prioritization happens in a spreadsheet and a meeting, with the loudest voice often winning over the clearest logic.
agents.tomorrow: The agent scores the backlog against stated OKRs and current capacity and shows its reasoning per item, giving the room a defensible starting ranking. The PM decides what's actually built.

payoff.note: Augments judgment rather than replacing labor; the win is better decisions, not fewer hours.

agents.name: Research Synthesis Agent
agents.agent_owns: Turning user interviews and session recordings into themed findings with evidence links.
agents.human_keeps: Validating the themes and their meaning.
agents.today: A researcher rewatches hours of sessions and hand-codes transcripts to find patterns. Synthesis is the slowest part of the cycle.
agents.tomorrow: The agent clusters interviews and recordings into candidate themes, each linked back to the exact moments that support it. The researcher validates or rejects each theme instead of building them from raw tape.

payoff.headline: ~50% of synthesis time reclaimed
not_captured: More studies become feasible when synthesis is cheap
Evidence links make findings harder to wave away

agents.name: Design Consistency Agent
agents.agent_owns: Checking new screens against the design system and flagging accessibility gaps.
agents.today: Design-system drift is caught late, in review or after ship, and accessibility issues slip through to QA or users.
agents.tomorrow: The agent checks each new screen against the design system and WCAG rules and flags deviations before review. The designer decides which are intentional exceptions and which are bugs.

payoff.headline: Fewer late-stage design reworks
assumptions: Catches drift at design time vs QA time
Rework late costs multiples of catching early
not_captured: Accessibility risk reduced before it reaches users
Design review focuses on craft, not policing

agents.name: Prototype Draft Agent
agents.agent_owns: Turning a spec into a wireframe draft checked against the design system.
agents.human_keeps: The craft and the final design.
agents.today: Turning a spec into a first wireframe is manual and slow, so early ideas stay stuck in words.
agents.tomorrow: The agent produces a first wireframe from the spec, already using design-system components, to react to. The designer owns everything past that first draft.

payoff.note: Speeds ideation; hard to attach a clean dollar figure without inventing one.

agents.name: Architecture Review Agent
agents.agent_owns: Reviewing proposed designs against standards and prior decisions (ADR-aware).
agents.today: Design reviews depend on whichever architect is in the room remembering why a past decision was made. Standards drift and past ADRs get re-litigated.
agents.tomorrow: The agent reviews a proposed design against your standards and the existing ADR history, flagging conflicts with past decisions before the meeting. The architect makes the ruling.

payoff.note: Judgment leverage and consistency; not a time-times-rate story.

agents.name: Decision Record Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting ADRs from design discussions and threads.
agents.today: ADRs are the documentation everyone agrees matters and nobody writes, so the 'why' behind decisions evaporates.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts an ADR from the actual design discussion, capturing options considered and the reasoning. The architect edits and ratifies.

payoff.headline: Institutional memory that would otherwise be lost
assumptions: Most ADRs never get written today
Cost of a lost decision surfaces months later as rework or repeated debate

agents.name: Dependency Map Agent
agents.agent_owns: Keeping the service dependency graph current from real traffic.
agents.human_keeps: Interpreting blast radius and what to do about it.
agents.today: The 'current' architecture diagram is always out of date, so blast-radius questions get answered from memory.
agents.tomorrow: The agent keeps the dependency graph live from actual traffic, so 'what breaks if this goes down' has a real-time answer. The human interprets and acts.

payoff.note: Risk reduction during incidents and planning; value is avoided outages, hard to pre-quantify honestly.

platforms.name: Poiesis
platforms.desc: Agentic SDLC. Six agents take a GitHub issue from intent to production, human in the loop.

platforms.name: Ichnos
platforms.desc: Provenance flight-recorder for AI-written code. A tamper-evident record for regulators.

agents.name: Code Review Agent
agents.agent_owns: First-pass PR review against your standards, with risk flags and suggested fixes.
agents.today: PRs wait on a busy senior engineer. Review quality swings with how tired the reviewer is, and the boring PRs get rubber-stamped.
agents.tomorrow: The agent does a first-pass review of every PR against your standards, flags risk, and suggests fixes, so the human reviewer starts from an annotated diff instead of a cold read. The agent never merges; a human approves.

payoff.headline: ~30-40% of review time reclaimed, faster PR cycle
assumptions: Senior reviewer spends ~4-6 hrs/week on PRs
not_captured: Faster cycle time is unblocked engineers

agents.name: Dependency Upgrade Agent
agents.agent_owns: Flagging stale or vulnerable dependencies, drafting the upgrade PR, running the tests.
agents.today: Dependency upgrades are deferred until a CVE forces a painful, rushed scramble.
agents.tomorrow: The agent flags stale and vulnerable dependencies, opens the upgrade PR, and runs the test suite so the change arrives pre-validated. A human approves the merge.

payoff.headline: Upgrade backlog kept current for cents of compute

agents.name: Migration Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting framework or API migrations one module at a time.
agents.today: Big migrations stall for quarters because nobody has a clear stretch of time to grind through them module by module.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts the migration one module at a time, each as a reviewable PR. The human reviews and merges each, so the migration moves in safe increments instead of stalling.

payoff.headline: Stalled migrations actually finish
not_captured: Staying on a supported framework is a security and hiring benefit

agents.name: Tech-Debt Triage Agent
agents.agent_owns: Scanning the codebase, ranking debt by risk versus effort, drafting remediation tickets.
agents.today: Tech debt is a vague anxiety with no shared list, so it competes badly against features and rarely wins.
agents.tomorrow: The agent scans the codebase, ranks debt by risk against effort, and drafts remediation tickets, turning anxiety into a prioritized list. The human decides what gets scheduled.

payoff.note: Improves decisions about debt; the payoff is avoided future failure, not current hours.

agents.name: Test Generation Agent
agents.agent_owns: Writing test cases from a spec or PR and flagging the paths nothing covers.
agents.today: Tests are written last, under deadline, so coverage is thin exactly where the risky, untested paths live.
agents.tomorrow: The agent generates test cases from the spec or PR and explicitly flags the paths nothing covers. The human judges whether the coverage is sufficient to ship.

payoff.headline: ~40-60% of test-writing time reclaimed
assumptions: Test authoring is a large share of feature time
not_captured: Coverage gaps made visible instead of assumed away

agents.name: Bug Triage Agent
agents.agent_owns: Deduping, attempting repro, ranking severity, and routing to the right team.
agents.human_keeps: Confirming severity on the ambiguous ones.
agents.today: New bugs pile into a queue where duplicates, mis-routes, and wrong severities waste the first hour of every triage.
agents.tomorrow: The agent dedupes, attempts a repro, assigns a draft severity, and routes to the owning team. A human confirms severity on the genuinely ambiguous cases.

payoff.headline: ~1 hr/day of triage returned per team
assumptions: Manual triage ~1-2 hrs/day across a team

agents.name: Regression Bisect Agent
agents.agent_owns: Correlating a new failure to the commit that most likely caused it.
agents.today: When a test starts failing, an engineer manually bisects through commits, a slow, frustrating hunt that blocks the pipeline.
agents.tomorrow: The agent correlates the new failure to the most likely offending commit and presents the evidence. The engineer confirms root cause instead of hunting for it.

payoff.headline: Hours per regression collapsed to minutes
assumptions: Agent narrows to a prime suspect immediately

agents.name: Release Readiness Agent
agents.agent_owns: Assembling a go/no-go signal from tests, coverage, and open criticals.
agents.today: Release readiness is a scramble of checking five dashboards and hoping nobody forgot a critical.
agents.tomorrow: The agent assembles the readiness signal, tests, coverage, open criticals, into one view with a recommendation. A human makes the actual go/no-go call.

payoff.note: Decision support; value is avoided bad releases, not clean hours saved.

agents.name: Change Risk Agent
agents.agent_owns: Scoring each change by blast radius and history, flagging the risky ones for review.
agents.today: Change advisory boards review everything equally, so routine changes waste time and genuinely risky ones don't get extra scrutiny.
agents.tomorrow: The agent scores each change by blast radius and past incident correlation and flags only the risky ones for board attention. Humans approve the high-risk changes; the rest flow.

payoff.headline: CAB time focused where risk actually is
assumptions: Most changes are low-risk but reviewed anyway
Agent lets routine changes bypass heavy review

agents.name: Release Notes Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting customer-facing release notes from merged work.
agents.today: Release notes are written at the last minute, so they're either skipped or read like a raw changelog.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts customer-facing notes from the merged PRs, in your voice, grouped by theme. The PM approves the story before it ships.

payoff.headline: ~2-3 hours saved per release

agents.name: Rollback Decision Support Agent
agents.agent_owns: Assembling the evidence during a bad deploy: error rates, affected users, blast radius.
agents.today: During a bad deploy, someone frantically pulls up dashboards to decide whether to roll back, under pressure, with partial information.
agents.tomorrow: The agent assembles the rollback evidence, error rates, affected users, scope, into one view the moment things look wrong. A human decides whether to roll back.

payoff.note: Value is faster, better incident decisions; framed as risk reduction, not hours.

platforms.name: Aegis
platforms.desc: Agentic DevSecOps. Rotates exposed secrets, blocks merges on critical CVEs, fixes IaC misconfigurations.

agents.name: Incident Commander Agent
agents.agent_owns: Assembling the timeline, correlating signals, drafting the running incident summary.
agents.today: During an incident, someone has to both fight the fire and keep a coherent timeline and comms going, and one of the two always suffers.
agents.tomorrow: The agent assembles the timeline, correlates signals across systems, and maintains the running incident summary in real time. The humans make every operational call; the agent keeps the record straight.

payoff.headline: Faster resolution, cleaner comms during incidents
assumptions: Coordination overhead is a real slice of MTTR
not_captured: Shorter outages are direct revenue and trust

agents.name: Postmortem Drafting Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting a blameless postmortem from the incident timeline.
agents.human_keeps: The lessons and the action items.
agents.today: Postmortems get written days later from patchy memory, or not at all, so lessons don't stick.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts a blameless postmortem straight from the incident timeline while it's fresh. The team owns the lessons and the actions.

payoff.headline: ~3-4 hours saved per incident, and they actually get written
not_captured: Lessons captured that would otherwise be lost

agents.name: Alert Noise Agent
agents.agent_owns: Clustering and deduping alerts, proposing threshold changes.
agents.today: On-call drowns in alert noise, so real signals get missed and everyone's numb to the pager.
agents.tomorrow: The agent clusters and dedupes alerts and proposes threshold changes to cut the noise. A human approves the tuning.

payoff.headline: Alert volume cut sharply, less on-call burnout
assumptions: Large fraction of alerts are noise/duplicates

platforms.name: Pistis
platforms.desc: Agentic IAM. Context-aware access that reasons about intent and risk instead of static role rules.

agents.name: Threat Model Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting a threat model from an architecture diagram or a PR.
agents.today: Threat modeling is a heavy workshop that happens rarely, so most changes ship without one.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts a threat model from the architecture or the PR, giving security a starting artifact for every meaningful change. The security engineer validates and extends it.

payoff.headline: Threat modeling on every change, not just the big ones
assumptions: Manual threat model is hours of senior time
Applied far more broadly than today
not_captured: Security scales past the size of the team

agents.name: Vulnerability Triage Agent
agents.agent_owns: Deduping scanner output and ranking by real exploitability and reachability, not raw CVSS.
agents.today: Scanners dump thousands of findings ranked by raw CVSS, so the team drowns in criticals that aren't actually reachable and misses the ones that are.
agents.tomorrow: The agent dedupes scanner output and re-ranks by real exploitability and reachability in your environment, not just CVSS. A human confirms the priority order before work is assigned.

payoff.headline: Security effort aimed at real risk, not CVSS noise
assumptions: Most raw criticals are not reachable
Manual triage of scanner output is days of senior time
Agent cuts the list to what matters

agents.name: Access Recertification Agent
agents.agent_owns: Flagging over-privileged and stale access for review.
agents.today: Access recerts are a dreaded quarterly spreadsheet where managers rubber-stamp everything to make it stop.
agents.tomorrow: The agent flags over-privileged and stale access continuously, so recert is a short list of real anomalies instead of a wall of everything. The human makes the recert decision.

payoff.headline: Recert becomes minutes of real decisions
assumptions: Blanket recerts waste manager time and rubber-stamp risk
not_captured: Standing access risk reduced continuously, not quarterly

agents.xref: Pistis grants access live; this cleans up what's already granted.

platforms.name: Nomos
platforms.desc: Agentic compliance. Maps every deployment to the control framework and delivers a signed evidence package to the auditor.

agents.name: Framework Gap Agent
agents.agent_owns: Running a pre-assessment against a new framework and flagging the gaps.
agents.today: Adopting a new framework (say, FedRAMP) starts with a manual, months-long gap assessment by expensive consultants.
agents.tomorrow: The agent runs a first-pass gap assessment against the new framework and produces a prioritized gap list. The compliance lead scopes the program from there.

payoff.headline: Weeks of consultant gap-assessment compressed
assumptions: Manual gap assessment is a costly multi-week engagement
not_captured: Faster path to a certification that unlocks deals

agents.name: Vendor Security Review Agent
agents.agent_owns: First-pass review of vendor SOC 2 reports and security questionnaires.
agents.today: Someone reads every vendor's SOC 2 and questionnaire by hand, a slow bottleneck that holds up procurement.
agents.tomorrow: The agent does a first-pass review of the vendor's SOC 2 and questionnaire, flagging exceptions and gaps against your bar. A human accepts or rejects the residual risk.

payoff.headline: ~2-4 hours saved per vendor review

agents.name: Pipeline Health Agent
agents.agent_owns: Monitoring jobs for failures, drift, and cost anomalies, and drafting the fix.
agents.today: Data pipelines fail silently and the first sign is a broken dashboard or a surprise bill.
agents.tomorrow: The agent monitors jobs for failures, drift, and cost spikes and drafts the fix when something breaks. An engineer approves before it's applied.

payoff.headline: Failures caught before dashboards break
assumptions: Silent failures cost analyst and engineer time downstream
not_captured: Bad decisions on stale data avoided

agents.name: Schema Change Impact Agent
agents.agent_owns: Flagging downstream breakage before a schema change ships.
agents.today: A schema change ships and quietly breaks three dashboards and a model nobody remembered depended on it.
agents.tomorrow: The agent traces the change through the lineage and flags everything downstream that will break, before it ships. The engineer decides sequencing and coordination.

payoff.note: Prevents breakage; value is avoided incidents, hard to quantify cleanly.

agents.name: Data Contract Agent
agents.agent_owns: Enforcing producer/consumer contracts and flagging violations at PR time.
agents.today: Producer and consumer teams break each other's assumptions constantly because the 'contract' lives in tribal knowledge.
agents.tomorrow: The agent enforces the data contracts and flags violations at PR time, before they ship. The teams negotiate any real exceptions.

payoff.note: Reduces cross-team breakage; risk reduction rather than a time figure.

platforms.name: Nexus
platforms.desc: Agentic internal support. Resolves associate requests across Jira, GitHub, Artifactory, Jenkins, and Ansible in plain English.

agents.name: Escalation Drafting Agent
agents.agent_owns: Turning a messy customer ticket into a clean, engineering-ready bug report.
agents.human_keeps: Approving before it hits the eng queue.
agents.today: Support escalations reach engineering as vague, half-reproducible tickets, and half the back-and-forth is just extracting the actual problem.
agents.tomorrow: The agent turns the messy customer ticket into a structured, repro-focused bug report with the missing details requested up front. A human approves before it enters the eng queue.

payoff.headline: Less eng time lost to ticket clarification
assumptions: Round-trips to clarify tickets cost eng and support time

agents.name: Support-to-Product Signal Agent
agents.agent_owns: Clustering tickets into product-feedback themes for PM.
agents.today: The richest source of product feedback, the support queue, is a firehose nobody has time to synthesize.
agents.tomorrow: The agent clusters tickets into recurring product-feedback themes and quantifies each. The PM interprets and prioritizes.

payoff.note: Turns noise into signal; value is better roadmap decisions, not saved hours.

agents.name: Asset Lifecycle Agent
agents.agent_owns: Tracking hardware and license lifecycle, flagging renewals and idle licenses.
agents.today: Nobody has a clean view of who has what license, so the company pays for idle seats and scrambles at renewal.
agents.tomorrow: The agent tracks the hardware and license estate, flags idle seats and upcoming renewals, and drafts the reclaim or renewal action. A human approves.

payoff.headline: Idle-license spend recovered
assumptions: Unused SaaS seats are a well-known 20-30% of spend
Agent flags idle seats for reclaim

agents.name: Ticket Deflection Agent
agents.agent_owns: Resolving common IT requests (password, access, software) end to end.
agents.today: IT spends its day on password resets and access requests instead of anything strategic.
agents.tomorrow: The agent resolves the common, well-defined IT requests end to end and escalates only the exceptions. Humans handle the genuinely unusual cases.

payoff.headline: Large share of L1 IT tickets deflected
assumptions: A big fraction of IT tickets are routine and repetitive
Agent resolves those end to end

agents.xref: Internal-IT counterpart to Nexus, which targets the developer toolchain.

platforms.name: Alethinos
platforms.desc: Agentic project intelligence. Reads every ticket, commit, and comment to surface why deliverables are at risk.

agents.name: Capacity Planning Agent
agents.agent_owns: Forecasting team capacity against committed work and flagging overcommitment early.
agents.today: Overcommitment is discovered mid-sprint, when it's already too late to do anything graceful about it.
agents.tomorrow: The agent forecasts capacity against committed work and flags overcommitment before the sprint starts. The engineering manager rebalances.

payoff.note: Better planning decisions; value is avoided slips, not clean hours saved.

agents.name: Onboarding DevEx Agent
agents.agent_owns: Answering 'how do I get access to X' and 'why did my deploy fail' without a ticket.
agents.today: New engineers lose their first weeks pinging people for access and deciphering cryptic build failures.
agents.tomorrow: The agent answers the routine 'how do I' and 'why did my deploy fail' questions instantly, from your actual toolchain. Humans take the true edge cases.

payoff.headline: Faster time-to-first-commit for new hires
assumptions: Onboarding friction costs days of new-hire and mentor time
not_captured: Senior engineers freed from repetitive questions

agents.name: Competitive Landscape Agent
agents.agent_owns: Continuously tracking competitor moves, pricing, hiring, and launches, and flagging material shifts.
agents.human_keeps: Interpreting what it means and deciding the response.
agents.today: Competitive intelligence is a slide someone refreshes before a board meeting, already stale on arrival.
agents.tomorrow: The agent tracks competitors continuously and flags material shifts as they happen. Strategy interprets and decides how to respond.

payoff.note: Judgment and timing value; not a time-times-rate figure.

agents.name: Scenario Modeling Agent
agents.agent_owns: Building and stress-testing strategic scenarios against historical and market data.
agents.today: Scenario work is a bespoke analyst effort per question, so only a few options ever get modeled.
agents.tomorrow: The agent builds and stress-tests scenarios, market entry, pricing, M&A, against the data, so more options get a real look. Leadership picks the path.

payoff.note: Decision-quality leverage, not labor replacement.

agents.name: M&A Screening Agent
agents.agent_owns: Scanning for acquisition targets against criteria and drafting first-pass diligence summaries.
agents.today: Target screening is a slow, relationship-driven process that misses non-obvious candidates.
agents.tomorrow: The agent scans for targets against your criteria and drafts first-pass diligence summaries. Corp dev decides what to actually pursue.

payoff.note: Value is deal quality and coverage, not hours.

agents.name: OKR Cascade Agent
agents.agent_owns: Checking whether team OKRs ladder up to company OKRs and flagging orphans and contradictions.
agents.today: OKRs are set in silos, so nobody notices that two teams' goals conflict or that some work ladders up to nothing.
agents.tomorrow: The agent checks team OKRs against company OKRs and flags orphaned or contradictory goals. Leadership resolves them.

payoff.note: Alignment value; avoided wasted effort rather than a clean figure.

agents.name: Content Production Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting posts, landing pages, and campaign copy in brand voice.
agents.today: Content is the perennial bottleneck; the calendar is always ahead of what the team can write.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts posts, landing pages, and campaign copy in your brand voice from a brief. A human edits and approves before anything publishes.

payoff.headline: ~50-70% of first-draft time reclaimed
assumptions: Drafting is the slow step, not ideation or editing
Agent produces the first draft, human polishes

agents.name: Campaign Performance Agent
agents.agent_owns: Monitoring live spend across channels and proposing reallocation.
agents.today: Budget shifts happen weekly, in a meeting, days after the data said to move.
agents.tomorrow: The agent monitors campaigns across channels and proposes reallocating spend toward what's working, in near real time. A human signs off on the moves.

payoff.headline: Wasted ad spend cut by faster reallocation
assumptions: Lag between signal and action wastes budget
Even a few % of spend is material

agents.name: SEO & Competitive Content Agent
agents.agent_owns: Tracking keyword gaps and competitor content moves, drafting the content calendar.
agents.today: SEO strategy is a periodic audit, so the calendar chases last quarter's gaps.
agents.tomorrow: The agent tracks keyword gaps and competitor content continuously and drafts a data-backed calendar. The marketer sets direction.

payoff.note: Value is traffic and positioning, hard to attribute cleanly to the agent.

agents.name: Brand Consistency Agent
agents.agent_owns: Scanning outbound material for tone, terminology, and visual violations before publish.
agents.today: Brand drift is caught by whoever happens to notice, after it's already gone out.
agents.tomorrow: The agent scans outbound material against brand guidelines before publish and flags tone, terminology, and visual violations. A human rules on the edge cases.

payoff.note: Brand-risk reduction; not a time figure.

agents.name: Press Monitoring Agent
agents.agent_owns: Tracking coverage and sentiment and flagging stories gaining velocity.
agents.today: PR finds out a story is blowing up when someone forwards it, hours too late.
agents.tomorrow: The agent tracks coverage and sentiment and flags stories gaining velocity early. Comms decides whether and how to respond.

payoff.note: Early-warning value; risk reduction, not hours.

agents.name: Statement Drafting Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting press statements and holding lines from approved positions.
agents.today: Under a deadline, statements get drafted from scratch in a scramble.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts statements and holding lines from your approved position library, fast, when time is short. The comms lead approves every word before it goes out.

payoff.note: Speed under pressure; value is reputational, not a clean figure.

agents.name: Crisis Signal Agent
agents.agent_owns: Watching social, reviews, and forums for early signals of a brewing issue.
agents.today: Brewing issues are visible in the data long before anyone connects the dots.
agents.tomorrow: The agent watches the signals and surfaces a brewing issue before it becomes a crisis. A human owns the crisis call.

payoff.note: Risk reduction; avoided-crisis value can't be honestly pre-quantified.

agents.name: Lead Qualification Agent
agents.agent_owns: Scoring and routing inbound leads and drafting first-touch outreach.
agents.today: Reps spend the top of every day sorting leads and writing cold first-touches instead of selling.
agents.tomorrow: The agent scores and routes inbound leads and drafts a tailored first-touch for each. The rep approves and sends, and spends the reclaimed time on live deals.

payoff.headline: ~1 selling hour/day returned per rep
not_captured: Faster lead response wins more deals

agents.name: Proposal / RFP Response Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting proposals and RFP answers from the knowledge base.
agents.today: RFPs eat days of copy-pasting from old responses and chasing SMEs for the same answers again.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts proposal and RFP responses from your knowledge base, pulling the right prior answers automatically. A human edits, prices, and sends.

payoff.headline: ~2-4 days saved per major RFP
assumptions: Agent drafts from the knowledge base
not_captured: More RFPs answered means more pipeline

agents.name: Deal Risk Agent
agents.agent_owns: Flagging stalling deals from activity patterns and suggesting next best action.
agents.today: Deals quietly stall and nobody notices until the forecast misses.
agents.tomorrow: The agent flags stalling deals from activity patterns and suggests a next action. The rep owns the play.

payoff.note: Value is won deals, not saved hours.

agents.name: Sales Call Intelligence Agent
agents.agent_owns: Summarizing calls, extracting commitments and objections, updating the CRM.
agents.today: CRM hygiene is the task reps hate and skip, so the pipeline data is always half-wrong.
agents.tomorrow: The agent summarizes each call, extracts commitments and objections, and updates the CRM automatically. The rep confirms.

payoff.headline: ~30-45 min/day of admin returned per rep

agents.name: Partner Performance Agent
agents.agent_owns: Tracking partner-sourced pipeline and delivery quality, flagging underperforming agreements.
agents.today: Partner performance is reviewed once a quarter, so underperforming agreements linger.
agents.tomorrow: The agent tracks partner-sourced pipeline and delivery quality continuously and flags agreements that are underperforming. The human handles the relationship.

payoff.note: Relationship and revenue value; not a time figure.

agents.name: Co-sell Coordination Agent
agents.agent_owns: Matching partner capabilities to open opportunities and drafting intro briefs.
agents.today: Co-sell matches depend on someone remembering which partner fits which deal.
agents.tomorrow: The agent matches partner capabilities to open opportunities and drafts the intro brief. A human makes the actual connection.

payoff.note: Revenue enablement; hard to attribute to the agent cleanly.

agents.name: Tier-1 Resolution Agent
agents.agent_owns: Resolving common customer tickets end to end and escalating the ambiguous ones.
agents.human_keeps: Escalations and anything with a relationship attached.
agents.today: Support teams spend most of their capacity on the same routine questions, so complex tickets wait.
agents.tomorrow: The agent resolves the common, well-understood tickets end to end and escalates anything ambiguous. Humans own escalations and relationship-sensitive cases.

payoff.headline: Large share of tier-1 volume deflected
assumptions: A big fraction of tickets are routine and repetitive
not_captured: Human agents focus on hard, high-value cases

agents.name: Churn Risk Agent
agents.agent_owns: Flagging at-risk accounts from usage and sentiment and drafting the save-play.
agents.today: Churn is usually noticed at renewal, when it's too late to save the account.
agents.tomorrow: The agent flags at-risk accounts from usage and sentiment signals early and drafts a save-play. The CSM runs the conversation.

payoff.note: Retained revenue is the value; framed as risk reduction, not hours.

agents.name: Onboarding Agent
agents.agent_owns: Guiding new customers through setup and escalating when a step fails repeatedly.
agents.today: Onboarding is high-touch and doesn't scale, so either CSMs drown or customers are left to flounder.
agents.tomorrow: The agent guides customers through setup and escalates only when a step repeatedly fails. Humans handle the genuinely stuck cases.

payoff.headline: CSM time focused on stuck customers, not happy-path setup
assumptions: Much onboarding is repeatable happy-path guidance

agents.name: Knowledge Base Maintenance Agent
agents.agent_owns: Detecting stale or contradicted docs and drafting updates.
agents.today: The knowledge base rots because keeping it current is nobody's actual job.
agents.tomorrow: The agent detects when docs are stale or contradicted by recent tickets and drafts the update. A human approves publish.

payoff.note: Deflection quality improves; indirect value.

platforms.name: Optikon
platforms.desc: AI spend visibility. Cost allocation and variance across every LLM provider, every dollar traced to an owner.

agents.name: Close Automation Agent
agents.agent_owns: Reconciling accounts and flagging anomalies for the accountant.
agents.today: Month-end close is a multi-day grind of reconciliation, where the accountant hunts for the one number that's off.
agents.tomorrow: The agent reconciles the accounts and surfaces the anomalies, so the accountant investigates a short list instead of hunting through everything. A human signs the close.

payoff.headline: ~2-4 days off the monthly close
assumptions: Reconciliation is a large share of close time
Agent does the matching, flags exceptions

agents.name: Expense Audit Agent
agents.agent_owns: Reviewing expense reports against policy, auto-approving clean ones, escalating exceptions.
agents.today: Expense review is either a rubber stamp or a bottleneck, and policy is applied inconsistently.
agents.tomorrow: The agent reviews every report against policy, auto-approves the clean ones, and escalates only exceptions. A human rules on those.

payoff.headline: Most expense reports clear without human touch
assumptions: Agent handles those, escalates the rest

agents.name: Invoice Processing Agent
agents.agent_owns: Extracting, matching, and coding invoices.
agents.today: AP keys invoices by hand and chases POs to match them, slow and error-prone.
agents.tomorrow: The agent extracts, matches, and codes invoices automatically. A human approves the payment run.

payoff.headline: ~70-80% of manual AP handling removed
assumptions: Manual keying + matching is minutes per invoice at volume

agents.name: FP&A Narrative Agent
agents.agent_owns: Turning actuals into a written variance story.
agents.today: FP&A spends days turning the variance numbers into the written story leadership actually reads.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts the variance narrative from the actuals, explaining the drivers in prose. The finance lead owns the final story to leadership.

payoff.headline: ~1-2 days saved per reporting cycle
assumptions: Narrative writing is a meaningful slice of the cycle

agents.name: Treasury & Cash Agent
agents.agent_owns: Monitoring cash position and forecast, flagging covenant or liquidity risks.
agents.today: Cash forecasting lives in a fragile spreadsheet, and covenant risk is spotted late.
agents.tomorrow: The agent monitors the cash position and forecast and flags covenant or liquidity risks early. The treasurer decides the moves.

payoff.note: Risk reduction on liquidity; avoided-cost value, not clean hours.

agents.name: Tax Provision Agent
agents.agent_owns: Assembling provision workpapers from the ledger and flagging positions needing judgment.
agents.today: Tax provision is a periodic scramble to assemble workpapers from the ledger by hand.
agents.tomorrow: The agent assembles the provision workpapers from the ledger and flags the positions that need judgment. The tax lead decides those.

payoff.headline: Days of workpaper assembly compressed
not_captured: More time for actual tax strategy

agents.name: Vendor Evaluation Agent
agents.agent_owns: Scoring RFP responses against weighted criteria and drafting the committee comparison.
agents.today: Vendor selection means manually scoring long RFP responses in a spreadsheet, inconsistently.
agents.tomorrow: The agent scores RFP responses against the weighted criteria and drafts a side-by-side comparison for the committee. The committee decides.

payoff.headline: ~1-2 days saved per selection
assumptions: Manual scoring of responses is multi-day

agents.name: Contract Renewal Agent
agents.agent_owns: Tracking renewals, flagging price hikes and unused seats, drafting renegotiation points.
agents.today: Renewals sneak up and auto-renew at a price increase before anyone reviews them.
agents.tomorrow: The agent tracks upcoming renewals, flags price hikes and unused seats, and drafts renegotiation talking points. A human negotiates.

payoff.headline: Renewal overspend caught before it auto-renews
assumptions: Silent auto-renewals at higher prices are common
Agent surfaces them with leverage points

agents.name: Spend Classification Agent
agents.agent_owns: Categorizing procurement spend and flagging maverick or off-contract buying.
agents.today: Spend categorization is manual and lagging, so off-contract buying hides in the noise.
agents.tomorrow: The agent categorizes spend continuously and flags maverick or off-contract purchases. A human addresses the behavior.

payoff.note: Savings depend on enforcement; indirect value.

agents.name: Policy Q&A Agent
agents.agent_owns: Answering benefits, leave, and policy questions from the handbook.
agents.today: HR fields the same handbook questions all day, so real people work waits.
agents.tomorrow: The agent answers routine benefits, leave, and policy questions from the handbook instantly. HR handles the edge cases.

payoff.headline: Routine HR questions deflected
assumptions: A large share of HR queries are handbook-answerable
not_captured: HR freed for real people work

agents.name: Onboarding Logistics Agent
agents.agent_owns: Prepping equipment, access, and paperwork, and tracking completion.
agents.today: New-hire setup is a checklist that lives in someone's head, so things slip and day one is rough.
agents.tomorrow: The agent preps equipment, access, and paperwork, tracks completion, and escalates gaps. A human owns the human part of the welcome.

payoff.headline: Smoother day one, less coordinator time
assumptions: Onboarding coordination is hours per hire

agents.name: Attrition Risk Agent
agents.agent_owns: Flagging flight-risk signals from engagement and performance data.
agents.human_keeps: The conversation — never an automated action.
agents.today: Regretted attrition is a surprise; the signals were there but scattered.
agents.tomorrow: The agent flags flight-risk signals for a manager's attention. The manager has the conversation. This never triggers an automated action, only a human one.

payoff.note: Retention value, and a sensitive area where over-quantifying would be inappropriate.

agents.name: Performance Cycle Agent
agents.agent_owns: Assembling review packets: goals, feedback, peer input.
agents.today: Review season means managers manually gathering goals, feedback, and peer input from five systems.
agents.tomorrow: The agent assembles the review packet from all the sources into one place. The manager writes the actual assessment.

payoff.headline: Hours per review returned to managers
assumptions: Times team size, twice a year
not_captured: Managers spend time on quality feedback, not gathering

agents.name: Resume Screening Agent
agents.agent_owns: Shortlisting candidates against role criteria with stated reasoning.
agents.human_keeps: Reviewing the shortlist; humans always interview.
agents.today: Recruiters skim hundreds of resumes fast and inconsistently, so good candidates get missed and bias creeps in.
agents.tomorrow: The agent shortlists against the role criteria and shows its reasoning per candidate. The recruiter reviews the shortlist, and humans do all interviewing and hiring decisions.

payoff.headline: Hours of screening per role reclaimed
assumptions: Screening hundreds of resumes is hours per role
Agent shortlists with rationale, recruiter reviews

agents.name: Interview Logistics Agent
agents.agent_owns: Scheduling, panel assembly, and reminder sequencing.
agents.today: Coordinating interview panels across calendars is a maddening time sink for recruiters.
agents.tomorrow: The agent handles scheduling, assembles the panel, and sequences reminders. The recruiter handles genuine conflicts.

payoff.headline: ~30-45 min saved per interview loop
assumptions: Scheduling coordination is significant per loop
not_captured: Faster loops mean better candidate experience

agents.name: JD Drafting Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting role descriptions calibrated against market postings.
agents.today: Job descriptions are copied from an old one and lightly edited, so they're stale and off-market.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts a JD calibrated against current market postings for the role. The hiring manager owns the bar and the final wording.

payoff.note: Quality and speed of hiring; not a clean time figure.

agents.name: Contract Redline Agent
agents.agent_owns: First-pass review against the negotiating playbook, precedent-aware severity, drafting redlines.
agents.human_keeps: Whether to accept, negotiate, or walk.
agents.today: A vendor contract lands and a lawyer reads the whole agreement to find what's off, roughly an hour per contract, and the nested carve-out in a boring section is exactly where things slip.
agents.tomorrow: The agent takes the first pass in seconds and hands counsel a brief: the deviations, ranked, each with suggested language and what this vendor accepted before. Counsel opens the contract already knowing the shape of the negotiation, then makes the calls. The agent flags and suggests; it never accepts, sends, or negotiates.

payoff.headline: ~$90/contract, ~$90K/year, plus the risk it catches
assumptions: ~60 min skilled review today, ~15 with the agent
not_captured: One missed carve-out or under-floor cap can dwarf a year of time savings

agents.repo_note: This one is built and runnable — link the repo and the sample brief. Full card already authored in agent-card-preview-v2.html; port it as-is.

agents.name: Regulatory Change Agent
agents.agent_owns: Monitoring regulatory updates and drafting impact summaries against current controls.
agents.today: Someone manually tracks regulatory feeds and tries to figure out what each change means for the company, slowly, and misses things.
agents.tomorrow: The agent monitors the relevant regulatory sources and drafts an impact summary for each change, mapped against your current controls. Legal makes the judgment call on what to do.

payoff.headline: Regulatory monitoring that doesn't depend on one person's vigilance
assumptions: Manual tracking is hours/week and error-prone
not_captured: A missed regulatory change is a compliance risk far larger than the time saved

agents.name: Policy Drift Agent
agents.agent_owns: Checking internal policies against current regulation and flagging drift.
agents.today: Internal policies quietly fall out of step with regulation until an audit finds the gap.
agents.tomorrow: The agent checks internal policies against current regulation and flags where they've drifted. A human decides the fix.

payoff.note: Compliance-risk reduction; avoided-penalty value, not clean hours.

agents.name: Litigation First-Pass Agent
agents.agent_owns: First-pass discovery review.
agents.today: Discovery review is armies of associates reading documents at enormous cost.
agents.tomorrow: The agent does a first-pass discovery review, surfacing and categorizing relevant documents. Counsel makes every actual call.

payoff.headline: First-pass review cost cut dramatically
assumptions: Manual first-pass discovery is a large, costly effort
not_captured: Consistency across a huge document set

agents.name: Controls Testing Agent
agents.agent_owns: Running continuous control checks instead of point-in-time sampling, and flagging failures.
agents.today: Controls are tested by sampling a few times a year, so failures live undetected in between.
agents.tomorrow: The agent runs continuous control checks against every relevant event and flags failures as they happen. The auditor validates and reports.

payoff.note: Coverage and risk reduction; the value is caught failures, not saved hours.

agents.name: Audit Evidence Agent
agents.agent_owns: Gathering evidence for auditors automatically from source systems.
agents.today: Audit season means weeks of screenshotting and exporting evidence by hand.
agents.tomorrow: The agent gathers the evidence from source systems automatically into an audit-ready package. The auditor scopes and judges.

payoff.headline: Weeks of evidence-gathering compressed
assumptions: Manual evidence collection is weeks per audit
Agent pulls from source systems automatically

agents.xref: Adjacent to Nomos, which owns tech compliance evidence; this is the internal-audit counterpart.

agents.name: Fraud Pattern Agent
agents.agent_owns: Flagging anomalous transaction patterns for investigation.
agents.today: Fraud is found after the fact, in a periodic review, once the money's gone.
agents.tomorrow: The agent flags anomalous transaction patterns as they occur. An investigator investigates.

payoff.note: Loss prevention; avoided-fraud value can't be honestly pre-quantified.

agents.name: Demand Forecasting Agent
agents.agent_owns: Predicting demand shifts and flagging anomalies.
agents.today: Demand forecasts are built in spreadsheets on a lagging cadence, so the plan is always fighting last month's reality.
agents.tomorrow: The agent predicts demand shifts from the live signals and flags anomalies for the planner. The planner approves the plan.

payoff.note: Value is better inventory and service levels; hard to attribute cleanly without real data.

agents.name: Inventory Optimization Agent
agents.agent_owns: Recommending reorder points and quantities.
agents.today: Reorder decisions are rules of thumb, so the company carries too much of some things and stocks out of others.
agents.tomorrow: The agent recommends reorder points and quantities from actual demand and lead times. A human approves the large orders.

payoff.note: Working-capital and stockout value; needs real data to quantify honestly.

agents.name: Supplier Risk Agent
agents.agent_owns: Monitoring supplier financial health and news for disruption risk.
agents.today: A key supplier's trouble is discovered when the shipment doesn't arrive.
agents.tomorrow: The agent monitors supplier health and news and flags disruption risk early. A human decides mitigation.

payoff.note: Disruption-avoidance value; risk reduction, not hours.

agents.name: Logistics Exception Agent
agents.agent_owns: Catching shipment delays early and proposing rerouting options.
agents.today: Delays are handled reactively, after they've already cascaded.
agents.tomorrow: The agent catches delays early and proposes rerouting options. A human approves the reroute.

payoff.note: Service-level value; avoided-cost, not clean hours.

agents.name: Ad Hoc Query Agent
agents.agent_owns: Answering business questions directly from the warehouse in natural language.
agents.today: Every business question becomes a ticket in the analyst queue, so simple answers take days.
agents.tomorrow: The agent answers business questions directly from the warehouse in natural language, so self-serve questions get instant answers. An analyst validates anything before it's used externally.

payoff.headline: Analyst queue freed from routine questions
assumptions: A large share of requests are simple, repeated lookups
Agent self-serves those, analyst validates external-facing ones

agents.name: Data Quality Agent
agents.agent_owns: Monitoring pipelines for schema drift, nulls, and anomalies.
agents.today: Data quality issues are discovered when a stakeholder notices a dashboard looks wrong.
agents.tomorrow: The agent monitors pipelines for drift, nulls, and anomalies and flags them before they reach a dashboard. A human fixes the root cause.

payoff.note: Prevents bad-data decisions; risk reduction rather than hours.

agents.name: Dashboard Integrity Agent
agents.agent_owns: Catching dashboards that no longer match the metric definitions.
agents.today: Metric definitions drift and dashboards quietly disagree with each other, eroding trust in all of them.
agents.tomorrow: The agent catches dashboards that have drifted from the canonical metric definitions and flags them. A human approves the correction.

payoff.note: Trust and consistency value; indirect.

agents.name: Space Utilization Agent
agents.agent_owns: Recommending seating and room allocation from badge and booking data.
agents.today: Space decisions are made on gut feel, so the company pays for room it doesn't use.
agents.tomorrow: The agent recommends seating and room allocation from actual badge and booking data. A human decides the floor plan.

payoff.note: Real-estate savings depend on action; indirect value.

agents.name: Maintenance Triage Agent
agents.agent_owns: Routing and prioritizing facilities requests.
agents.today: Facilities requests pile up and get handled in the order they're noticed, not by urgency.
agents.tomorrow: The agent routes and prioritizes facilities requests by urgency and type. A human handles disputes.

payoff.note: Efficiency gain; small and hard to quantify cleanly.

agents.name: Lease & Vendor Tracking Agent
agents.agent_owns: Flagging upcoming lease renewals and obligations.
agents.today: Lease obligations and renewal windows are tracked in a spreadsheet nobody checks until it's urgent.
agents.tomorrow: The agent tracks lease renewals and obligations and flags them ahead of the deadline. A human negotiates.

payoff.note: Avoided missed windows; indirect value.

agents.name: Board Pack Assembly Agent
agents.agent_owns: Pulling the standard board metrics package together.
agents.today: Every board cycle, someone spends days assembling the same metrics package from a dozen sources.
agents.tomorrow: The agent assembles the standard metrics package automatically each cycle. The exec adds the narrative and judgment that actually matters.

payoff.headline: ~1-2 days saved per board cycle
not_captured: Exec time spent on narrative, not gathering

agents.name: Investor Update Agent
agents.agent_owns: Drafting investor updates from metrics and highlights.
agents.today: Investor updates slip because writing them from scratch competes with running the company.
agents.tomorrow: The agent drafts the investor update from the metrics and highlights. The exec reviews and sends.

payoff.note: Consistency and cadence value; judgment work at the core.

agents.name: Meeting Synthesis Agent
agents.agent_owns: Turning exec meeting transcripts into decisions, owners, and follow-ups.
agents.today: Decisions made in exec meetings evaporate because nobody captured who owns what.
agents.tomorrow: The agent turns the meeting transcript into a clean list of decisions, owners, and follow-ups. The chief of staff confirms.

payoff.headline: Decisions and owners captured, not lost
assumptions: Manual minutes are inconsistent or skipped
not_captured: Follow-through improves when owners are explicit

agents.name: Comms Drafting Agent
agents.agent_owns: Adapting exec announcements into the right format for each channel and checking tone.
agents.today: An exec announcement gets manually reworked for email, Slack, and all-hands, each slightly different and slightly off.
agents.tomorrow: The agent adapts the announcement into the right format for each channel, in a consistent tone. Comms approves before it goes out.

payoff.note: Consistency and time value; small and hard to quantify precisely.

agents.name: Org Q&A Agent
agents.agent_owns: Answering 'where do I find X' questions from company knowledge.
agents.today: People ping HR, IT, and each other all day for things that are written down somewhere nobody can find.
agents.tomorrow: The agent answers 'where do I find X / who owns Y' from company knowledge instantly. Humans handle the genuinely new questions.

payoff.headline: Countless small interruptions deflected
assumptions: 'Where is X' questions are constant and cheap individually but huge in aggregate
